steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The task asks for the choice that best explains the disadvantage of using audio features to predict popularity. The key notes are: (1) audio-feature algorithms may fail to identify main features of songs with varying acoustic traits, and (2) tag-based algorithms (fixed traits like genre) are more reliable.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — Many popularity-predicting algorithms are based on a song's audio features, such as loudness and pitch intervals.: Describes what audio features are used for, not why they are a disadvantage. ✗
  • Choice B — Algorithms based on audio features may misidentify the main features of a song with varying acoustic traits, making such algorithms less reliable predictors of popularity than those based on fixed traits.: Names the specific failure (misidentification in varying-trait songs) and contrasts it with the more reliable fixed-trait algorithms — directly explaining the disadvantage. ✓
  • Choice C — Audio features describe acoustic traits such as pitch intervals, which may vary within a song, whereas descriptive tags describe fixed traits such as genre, which are reliable predictors of popularity.: Contrasts the two types of traits but never calls audio-feature algorithms unreliable or less accurate. ✗
  • Choice D — The MSD's descriptive tags are reliable predictors of a song's popularity, as the traits they describe are fixed.: Explains why tags work well, not why audio features fail. ✗
That matches Choice B — Algorithms based on audio features may misidentify the main features of a song with varying acoustic traits, making such algorithms less reliable predictors of popularity than those based on fixed traits..
